title: New DNA Study Changes How We Think About Where Humans Came From
summary: A new study about human origins says that early humans did not all come from one group in Africa. Instead, there were many groups of early humans living in different parts of Africa. They moved around and mixed with each other for a very long time. Scientists studied the DNA of people living today, especially the Nama people from southern Africa. The Nama have a lot of genetic variety. The study found that the first big split between early human groups happened about 120,000 to 135,000 years ago. Before that, different groups had been sharing genes for hundreds of thousands of years. This new idea is like a network instead of a simple family tree. It helps explain why humans have so much genetic diversity. The study was done by researchers at the University of California, Davis and McGill University. They say this changes our understanding of human evolution.

keywords.explanation: A molecule inside our cells that carries genetic information and determines traits like eye color.
People from whom we are descended, like great-grandparents.
Variety in genes among individuals in a population.
A group of people living in the same area.
Remains of ancient living things preserved in rock.
The process by which species change over time.

questions.question: What did the new study say about early humans?
options: They all came from one group in Africa.
They came from many groups that mixed together.
questions.correct_answer: They came from many groups that mixed together.

questions.question: Which people helped scientists learn about human origins?
When did the first big split between early human groups happen?
What did scientists study to learn about human origins?
What is the new model of human evolution like?
Where did the researchers who did the study work?

background_read: Humans are part of a species called Homo sapiens. Scientists have long thought that all modern humans came from a single population in Africa. This new study says that early humans were spread across Africa in different groups that mixed with each other. The study used DNA from the Nama people, who have lived in southern Africa for a long time and have a lot of genetic variety. By looking at their DNA, scientists could figure out how early human groups moved and mixed. This helps us understand why people today have different traits.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists at UC Davis and McGill University, led by Brenna Henn and Simon Gravel.
WHAT: A study that used DNA to show that early humans came from many groups in Africa that mixed together, not just one group.
WHERE: The study focused on human populations across Africa, especially the Nama people in southern Africa.
WHY: To better understand how modern humans evolved and why we have so much genetic diversity.

perspectives.description: All modern humans came from a single ancestral population in Africa.
Early humans were spread across Africa in multiple groups that mixed over time.
